methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 11 same-sector + similar-valuation-tier peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $1.0B - $23.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 24.4x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Ouster
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1.5B

private.name: Aeva Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Credo Semiconductor, a public company that went public via IPO in 2022, operates in the Chips & Semiconductors sector and currently trades at a valuation exceeding $5.0B. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of 11 same-sector peers spanning both private and public markets, including Series A through Series H stage companies and three public semiconductor benchmarks (ALAB, NVDA, AMD).

The peer-median method is the primary applicable triangulation technique. Across the 11-company comparable set—which includes Cerebras ($23.0B, Series H), PsiQuantum ($7.0B, Series E), Etched ($5.0B, Series A), SambaNova Systems ($5.0B, Series D), and Tenstorrent ($2.7B, Series D)—the median valuation is $2.3B, with a range spanning $1.0B to $23.0B. Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples (median 24.4x across the three public semiconductor tickers) cannot be applied, as target revenue is not disclosed. This is a standard constraint with public companies reporting limited forward-guidance in frontier semiconductor segments.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $2.0B to $2.7B (median $2.3B), materially below Credo's stated $5.0B+. Key uncertainties include undisclosed revenue and profitability metrics, the substantial valuation dispersion within the peer set (18x between min and max), and the heterogeneity of business models across AI accelerators, quantum, and specialized chip architectures. The wide peer range reflects early-stage market segmentation rather than consensus valuation anchors.
